augustine-restless-heart.text:
You have made us for yourself, O Lord, and our hearts are restless until they rest in you.

Great are you, O Lord, and greatly to be praised; great is your power, and of your wisdom there is no end. And man, being a part of your creation, desires to praise you — man, who bears about with him his mortality, the witness of his sin, even the witness that you resist the proud — yet man, this part of your creation, desires to praise you.

You move us to delight in praising you; for you have made us for yourself, and our hearts are restless until they rest in you.

Grant me, O Lord, to know and understand which is first — to call on you or to praise you; and likewise, whether to know you or to call upon you comes first. But who calls upon you without knowing you? For he who does not know you may call upon you as other than you are.

Or is it rather that we call on you in order that we may know you? But how shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? Or how shall they believe without a preacher?

They shall praise the Lord who seek him, for those who seek shall find him, and those who find shall praise him. Let me seek you, Lord, by calling on you, and let me call on you believing in you, for you have been preached to us.

augustine-late-have-i-loved.text:
Late have I loved you, O Beauty ever ancient, ever new, late have I loved you!

You were within me, but I was outside, and it was there that I searched for you. In my unloveliness I plunged into the lovely things which you created. You were with me, but I was not with you. Created things kept me from you; yet if they had not been in you they would not have been at all.

You called, you shouted, and you broke through my deafness. You flashed, you shone, and you dispelled my blindness. You breathed your fragrance on me; I drew in breath and now I pant for you. I have tasted you, now I hunger and thirst for more. You touched me, and I burned for your peace.

kempis-imitation-inward-life.text:
The kingdom of God is within you, says the Lord. Turn to the Lord with your whole heart, forsake this wretched world, and your soul shall find rest.

Learn to despise outward things and to give yourself to things inward, and you shall see the kingdom of God come within you. For the kingdom of God is peace and joy in the Holy Spirit, which is not given to the unholy.

Christ will come to you, offering his consolation, if you prepare a worthy dwelling within for him. All his glory and beauty is from within, and there he delights himself.

His visits with the inward man are frequent, his communication sweet, his consolation full of peace, his familiarity exceedingly wonderful.

Faithful soul, prepare your heart for this your Bridegroom, that he may come to you and dwell within you. For he says: If anyone loves me, he will keep my word, and my Father will love him, and we will come to him and make our home with him.

Give therefore room to Christ, and deny entrance to all others. When you have Christ, you are rich, and he is sufficient for you. He will be your provider and faithful helper in every need, so that you need not trust in men.

kempis-carrying-the-cross.text:
Jesus has now many lovers of his heavenly kingdom, but few bearers of his cross. He has many seekers of comfort, but few of tribulation. He finds many companions of his table, but few of his fasting.

All desire to rejoice with him, but few are willing to endure anything for his sake. Many follow Jesus to the breaking of bread, but few to the drinking of the cup of his passion.

Many reverence his miracles, but few follow the shame of his cross.

Many love Jesus as long as no adversity touches them. Many praise and bless him as long as they receive any comfort from him. But if Jesus hides himself and leaves them for a little while, they fall either into complaining or into too great dejection.

But they who love Jesus for the sake of Jesus, and not for some consolation of their own — they bless him in all tribulation and anguish of heart as well as in the state of highest comfort. And even if he should never give them consolation, they would still always praise him and always wish to give him thanks.

brother-lawrence-practice-presence.text:
The time of business does not with me differ from the time of prayer; and in the noise and clutter of my kitchen, while several persons are at the same time calling for different things, I possess God in as great tranquility as if I were upon my knees at the blessed sacrament.

We need not cry to him from a great distance. He is nearer to us than we are to ourselves.

It is not needful that we should be always in church to be with God. We may make a chapel of our heart, wherein to retire from time to time, to converse with him in meekness, humility, and love.

Every one is capable of such familiar conversation with God — some more, some less. He knows what we can do. Let us begin. Perhaps he is only waiting for one generous resolution on our part.

The most excellent method I found of going to God was that of doing my common business without any view of pleasing men, and, as far as I was capable, purely for the love of God.

That we ought not to be weary of doing little things for the love of God, who regards not the greatness of the work, but the love with which it is performed.

julian-all-shall-be-well.text:
And in this he showed me a little thing, the size of a hazelnut, in the palm of my hand, and it was as round as a ball. I looked at it with the eyes of my understanding and thought: What can this be? And it was answered generally thus: It is all that is made. I marveled how it could last, for I thought it might suddenly have fallen to nothing, it was so small.

And I was answered in my understanding: It lasts and ever shall, because God loves it. And so everything has its being by the love of God.

In this little thing I saw three properties. The first is that God made it, the second that God loves it, the third that God keeps it.

And all shall be well, and all shall be well, and all manner of thing shall be well.

He said not: You shall not be tempest-tossed, you shall not be work-weary, you shall not be discomforted. But he said: You shall not be overcome.

God wants us to know that he keeps us safe at all times, in sorrow and in joy. And sometimes a soul is left to itself for the profit of its sin. But all the while God guards us, and never does he leave us.

chrysostom-prayer-morning.text:
When you arise from your bed, before you do anything else, say: I thank you, Lord, for your goodness and patience, that you were not angry with me, a sinner, nor did you destroy me in my transgressions, but showed your usual love toward mankind, and raised me up as I lay in despair, to offer my morning worship.

Let the first movements of the soul be thanks and praise to God. Let the day begin with him, and let us give thanks that he has brought us safely to the light.

Prayer in the morning is the key of the day. It opens all its hours to the grace of God. The soul that begins with God finds God everywhere.

Do not say: I cannot pray in the morning because I have business to attend to. This very business is the reason you should pray. You are about to enter the world with all its confusion. How will you endure it without first gathering your strength in the presence of God?

The time you spend in prayer at the beginning of the day is not subtracted from your activities — it is multiplied into them.

bonhoeffer-psalms-prayerbook.text:
A psalm that we cannot utter as a prayer, that makes us falter and horrifies us, is a hint to us that here Someone else is praying, not we — that the One who is here protesting his innocence, who is invoking God's judgment, who has come to such infinite depths of suffering, is none other than Jesus Christ himself.

He it is who prays, and not only here but in the whole Psalter.

The Psalter is the prayer book of Jesus Christ in the truest sense of the word. He prayed the Psalter, and now it has become his prayer for all time.

When we pray the Psalms, we are joining in the prayer of Jesus Christ. His prayer becomes our prayer. His words carry us. When we can no longer pray, when our words run dry, the Psalms give us a voice.

The richness of the Word of God ought to determine our prayer, not the poverty of our heart. Thus if we want to pray with confidence and gladness, then the words of Holy Scripture will have to be the solid basis of our prayer.

Here we know that Jesus Christ prays with us, and that we can pray along with him — and so our prayer will be heard.

lewis-weight-of-glory.text:
If we consider the unblushing promises of reward and the staggering nature of the rewards promised in the Gospels, it would seem that Our Lord finds our desires not too strong, but too weak.

We are half-hearted creatures, fooling about with drink and ambition and pleasure when infinite joy is offered us, like an ignorant child who wants to go on making mud pies in a slum because he cannot imagine what is meant by the offer of a holiday at the sea.

We are far too easily pleased.

Indeed, if we consider the unblushing promises of reward promised in the Gospels, it would seem that our Lord finds our desires not too strong, but too weak.

At present we are on the outside of the world, the wrong side of the door. We discern the freshness and purity of morning, but they do not make us fresh and pure. We cannot mingle with the splendors we see.

But all the leaves of the New Testament are rustling with the rumor that it will not always be so. Some day, God willing, we shall get in.

athanasius-incarnation.text:
For the Son of God became man so that we might become God.

He, indeed, assumed humanity that we might become God. He manifested himself by means of a body in order that we might perceive the Mind of the unseen Father.

He endured the insolence of men that we might inherit immortality. For while he himself was in no way injured, being impassible and incorruptible and very Word and God, men who were suffering, and for whose sakes he endured all this, he maintained and preserved in his own impassibility.

For he was made man that we might be made God; and he manifested himself by a body that we might receive the idea of the unseen Father; and he endured the insolence of men that we might inherit immortality.

For while he himself was in no way injured — being impassible and incorruptible and very Word and God — men who were suffering, and for whose sakes he endured all this, he preserved and healed by his own body.

augustine-prayer-of-praise.text:
How shall I call upon my God, my God and Lord, since, when I call upon him, I shall be calling him to myself? And what room is there within me, where my God can come into me? Where can God come into me, God who made heaven and earth? Is there, indeed, O Lord my God, anything in me that can contain you?

Do heaven and earth, which you have made, and in which you have made me, contain you? Or since nothing that exists could exist without you, does everything that exists contain you?

Since, then, I too exist, why do I ask you to come into me, since I would not exist unless you were already in me? For I am not yet in hell, and yet you are there also. For if I go down to hell, you are there.

I could not be then, O my God, could not be at all, unless you were in me. Or rather, I could not be unless I were in you, of whom are all things, by whom are all things, in whom are all things.

kempis-on-patience.text:
My child, patience and humility in adversity are more pleasing to me than much devotion and consolation in prosperity.

Why does a small matter spoken against you make you sad? Even if it had been much more, you should not have been moved. But now let it pass. It is not the first, it is nothing new; and if you live long, it will not be the last.

You are brave enough as long as nothing stands in your way. You can give good advice and encourage others with your words; but when trouble unexpectedly knocks at your own door, your counsel and courage both fail.

Consider your great frailty, which you experience so often in small matters. Yet it is for your salvation when these and similar trials come upon you.

Put it out of your heart as best you can, and if tribulation has touched you, do not let it cast you down or entangle you for long. Bear it patiently at least, if you cannot bear it joyfully. Even if you are reluctant to bear it and feel indignation, restrain yourself, and allow no ill-considered word to escape you that might scandalize the weak.

teresa-avila-interior-castle.text:
I began to think of the soul as if it were a castle made of a single diamond or of very clear crystal, in which there are many rooms, just as in heaven there are many mansions.

Now if we think carefully over this, the soul of the righteous person is nothing but a paradise, in which, as God tells us, he takes his delight.

For what do you think a room will be like which is the delight of a King so mighty, so wise, so pure, so full of all that is good? I can find nothing with which to compare the great beauty of a soul and its great capacity.

It is no small pity that through our own fault we do not understand ourselves or know who we are. Would it not be a sign of great ignorance, if a person were asked who he was, and could not say, and had no idea who his father or his mother was, or from what country he came? Though that is great stupidity, our own is incomparably greater if we make no attempt to discover what we are, and only know that we are living in these bodies.

Let us now imagine that this castle has many mansions, some above, others below, others at each side; and in the center and midst of them all is the chiefest mansion, where the most secret things pass between God and the soul.

john-of-cross-dark-night.text:
One dark night, fired with love's urgent longings — ah, the sheer grace! — I went out unseen, my house being now all stilled.

In darkness, and secure, by the secret ladder, disguised — ah, the sheer grace! — in darkness and concealment, my house being now all stilled.

On that glad night, in secret, for no one saw me, nor did I look at anything, with no other light or guide than the one that burned in my heart.

God nurtures the soul in darkness, where no eye can see and no understanding can reach, in order to raise it to divine union. And this darkness is necessary because the light of God is so bright that it blinds the natural sight of the soul.

The soul must pass through this darkness in order to come to the admirable light of divine union. For just as the eyes of the bat are blinded by bright light, so also the intellect of the soul is blinded by the excessive brightness of divine light.

This dark night is an inflowing of God into the soul, which purges it of its habitual ignorances and imperfections, natural and spiritual. Contemplatives call it infused contemplation, or mystical theology, in which God secretly teaches the soul and instructs it in the perfection of love, without its doing anything or understanding how this is happening.

lewis-mere-christianity-hope.text:
Hope is one of the theological virtues. This means that a continual looking forward to the eternal world is not, as some modern people think, a form of escapism or wishful thinking, but one of the things a Christian is meant to do.

It does not mean that we are to leave the present world as it is. If you read history, you will find that the Christians who did most for the present world were just those who thought most of the next.

It is since Christians have largely ceased to think of the other world that they have become so ineffective in this. Aim at heaven and you will get earth thrown in. Aim at earth and you will get neither.

Most people, if they have really learned to look into their own hearts, would know that they do want, and want acutely, something that cannot be had in this world. There are all sorts of things in this world that offer to give it to you, but they never quite keep their promise.

If I find in myself a desire which no experience in this world can satisfy, the most probable explanation is that I was made for another world. If none of my earthly pleasures satisfy it, that does not prove that the universe is a fraud. Probably earthly pleasures were never meant to satisfy it, but only to arouse it, to suggest the real thing.

bonhoeffer-life-together.text:
It is not simply to be taken for granted that the Christian has the privilege of living among other Christians. Jesus Christ lived in the midst of his enemies. At the end all his disciples deserted him. On the cross he was utterly alone, surrounded by evildoers and mockers.

For this reason alone every Christian community must realize that not only do the weak need the strong, but also that the strong cannot exist without the weak. The elimination of the weak is the death of fellowship.

Christian brotherhood is not an ideal which we must realize; it is rather a reality created by God in Christ in which we may participate.

The first service that one owes to others in the fellowship consists in listening to them. Just as love to God begins with listening to his Word, so the beginning of love for our brothers and sisters is learning to listen to them.

Many people are looking for an ear that will listen. They do not find it among Christians, because these Christians are talking when they should be listening. He who can no longer listen to his brother will soon be no longer listening to God either.

bernard-loving-god.text:
You want me to tell you why God is to be loved and how much. I answer: the reason for loving God is God himself; and the measure of love due to him is immeasurable love.

Is this not enough? Perhaps it is, for the wise. But I am debtor to the unwise as well; and so where enough has been said for the wise, I must give attention to the others also.

God deserves to be loved very much by us, because he loved us first, he who is so great and who loves us so freely, small and poor as we are. This is why I said at the beginning that the measure of our love for God is to love him without measure.

For since our love is directed to God, who is infinite and immeasurable, the limit of that love — if there could be a limit — would be to love without limit.

Love is sufficient of itself; it gives pleasure by itself and because of itself. It is its own merit, its own reward. Love looks for no cause outside itself, no effect beyond itself. Its profit lies in the practice of it. I love because I love. I love in order that I may love.

chrysostom-thanksgiving.text:
In everything give thanks. What do you say? Even in affliction? Yes, for it is a medicine that destroys our sins and makes us righteous. If we knew the value of afflictions we would ask for them.

When therefore things happen that we do not wish, let us give thanks. For this is truly a cause for giving thanks: not only that we were created, not only that we were made rational beings, not only that we received the gift of life, but that we received also the gift of suffering.

Though it sounds strange to say it, affliction is a greater gift than creation. For what good would it do us to be created if we were lost? But affliction, rightly borne, leads us home.

Gratitude is not merely an emotion but a discipline. It is the practice of seeing God in all things — not only in the blessings we receive but in the trials that shape us. The grateful heart does not deny suffering; it refuses to let suffering have the last word.

Therefore let us give thanks for all things. This is the sacrifice most pleasing to God: a heart that praises him not only in prosperity but in adversity, not only when the way is clear but when the path is dark.

gregory-great-pastoral-care.text:
No one presumes to teach an art that he has not first mastered through study. How foolish therefore is it for the unskilled to assume pastoral authority, since the government of souls is the art of arts.

For what is the care of souls but the care of wounds invisible? And who would attempt to heal wounds he cannot see unless he has first learned the nature of the disease?

The leader should be discreet in keeping silence, profitable in speech; a near neighbor to everyone in sympathy, exalted above all in contemplation; a familiar friend of good livers through humility, unbending against the vices of evildoers through the zeal of righteousness.

The ruler should not diminish his attention to the inner life because of external occupations, nor should he relinquish his care for external matters because of anxieties about the inner life.

For it is often the case that some people, when they undertake the guidance of souls, become so absorbed in outward concerns that they neglect their own inner life. And again, others occupy themselves so exclusively with their inner life that they neglect the external duties that they owe to their neighbors.

spurgeon-morning-and-evening.text:
Let us begin the day by admiring the goodness of the Lord. Before the first rays of the sun streak the sky, let us lift up our hearts in gratitude. He who was watchful over us during the night has brought us to the light of another day.

New every morning is the love our awakening and uprising prove; through sleep and darkness safely brought, restored to life and power and thought.

Begin the day with God! He is your Sun and Shield, your shade by day, your light by night. Take to him your first waking thoughts. Let your heart's door stand open for the King to enter. Before the world rushes in with its demands and distractions, give the first fruits of your attention to the One who gave you breath.

The morning hour is the rudder of the day. As the first hour goes, so goes the rest. If you begin in communion with God, you carry that communion into every task and trial. If you begin in haste and anxiety, those companions will follow you through the day.

Do not say that you have no time. You have exactly as much time as all the saints who have ever lived. They found time because they made it their first priority. The time is there. The question is whether we will give it to the One who gave us everything.
